message: "@rectilinear-cinema, @yellow-humanist, Let's keep it civil, folks. We're all adults here.
"@random-anion, Oh look, a comedian! I haven't laughed this hard since I watched paint dry. 😂"

> Except that he wasn't harassing them

This isn't for you to decide, in a legal sense, it's for the court. You can't really call a court case frivolous without actually seeing all the evidence, and you won't see all the evidence unless you're acually there.

> But kids agitate for professors to be fired or campus presidents as well for the most preposterous reasons.

You've stepped back from what you were saying before here: you said they *were* fired. Find me an example of anyone fired for 'not agreeing enthusiastically with everything I said'.

> Holocaust denial laws, sure

I literally just pointed out why they're a terrible example. Seriously, supply the evidence that they're abused like you claim or don't use them as an example.

>  Marine Le Pen and her father faced prison time simply for saying words a few others did not like.

This is a blatant lie: she faced trial for 'incitement to discrimination'. She hasn't even been found guilty yet, she was simply accused of it.

This is all incredibly sensationalist. The case is Canada involves someone looking at prison time for *criminal harassment*. Your take on his guilt or innocence is irrelevant, it's up to the court to decide, as they are qualified to do. The crime isn't 'disagreeing with feminists'.

> for saying something that hurt someone's feewings

This is pure spin: they were fired in almost all instances because the university found they said something hateful. That's not merely 'hurting someone's feelings', and it's hardly the actions of a 'lunatic': institutions have been doing this for a very, very long time. Universities are completely within their right to fire employees or expel students who fail in their duty to the rest of the university, namely maintaining some baseline level of tolerance.

> Europeans go on trial and face fines or prison for saying things that someone disagrees with.

Do you mean holocaust denial laws? Can you find me one instance where someone has actually gone to prison for *merely* giving an opinion someone disagreed with without additional incitement, or libel, or some other crime?

"@pounded-hydrolysis, you're missing the point entirely. It's not about what's legal, it's about freedom of speech and thought. And @recent-marsh, don't be so naive, universities aren't exactly known for their tolerance of differing opinions these days."

The "superdelegates" argument was cited by Hillary Clinton supporters in 2008 as well, when she had a substantial lead early on. However, by the end of the campaign, Obama had gained the majority of superdelegates.

I think it's unlikely that there would be ever be a scenario where any candidate wins a clear majority of the popular vote in the primaries, but the superdelegates vote to allow the second place candidate to be nominated. It would open up the nominee (Clinton in that case) to charges of illegitimacy, particularly as Democrats have made voting rights and "the will of the people" a big issue, what with the Voting Rights Act, attempts to abolish the Electoral College, fighting against gerrymandering and against Voter ID laws. "The UnDemocratic Party" would be too easy a headline to write.

(For the 2008 precedent, see Nancy Pelosi's statement: ["If the votes of the superdelegates overturn what's happened in the elections," said Pelosi, "it would be harmful to the Democratic Party."'](http://blogs.abcnews.com/politicalradar/2008/03/pelosis-delegat.html))

As for the Democratic primary being an indicator of electability, they're not a particularly good one. Primary voters are almost all registered Democrats, a group significantly more liberal than voters as a whole. There have been a number of cases where a primary electorate has nominated a clearly weaker candidate than would be optimal for a general election. At the Presidential level, George McGovern lost in a landslide to Richard Nixon in 1972, when another candidate would likely have made the race much closer. For the Republicans, "Tea Party" candidates have been nominated and lost in races which they would likely have won otherwise, the most egregious example being the 2010 US Senate election in Delaware, in which the long-time moderate Congressman Mike Castle lost to the notorious Christine O'Donnell. Sanders being nominated could well be reminiscent of one of those cases.
We may not like her, but she does have qualities that will be very appealing next to a Republican. She's just not that appealing next to Bernie.

"@coral-pelican, you're acting like superdelegates are some mystical creature that only appear when it's convenient for your narrative. They've been a part of the process since the 70s, get over it. And @rectilinear-map, you're right, Hillary's not the most exciting candidate, but she's not the Wicked Witch of the West either. Bernie's a great grandpa, but he's not the only one who can light a fire under the Dems."
"@seasoned-wavelength, you're right, superdelegates aren't new, but using them to override the popular vote is like saying 'rules are for fools, unless we need them to win' 🙄"

message:
> That's my point exactly. Like I said, "we still can't reliably measure that, so we keep assigning people based on their genitals"

Ohh, I thought with measurable part of physical sex you meant some brain scan or something.

> The correlation proves that there is something biological going on somewhere, just like the index finger thing shows that there is something biological going on with sexual orientation.

No, not really, for all you know the same environmental factors that cause orientation also affect finger length and the same environmental factors that affect gender identity also influence how the BTSc is formed.

Volumes of areas in our brain are ultimately shaped by environmental factors during brain formatting.

It's entirely possible that there's a biological component, but these things don't prove it.

> For all I know, I could have two X chromosomes making me biologically female, but maybe my parents decided to make me a dude while I was still an infant. However, knowing that I'm biologically female, I wouldn't change anything about myself as I'm happy the way I am right now.

So far, anecdotal experience with incidents of kids who were raised  as the gender opposite of their sex, such as due to trying to cover up for losing the penis in a botched circumcision, tends to show that they do grow up identifying as "trans", that is, essentially along the lines of their their birth sex, rejecting the one assigned to them artificially. 

It is prudent to make a distinction between **gender identity**, and **gender expression**. 

Gender identity appears to be a measurable part of physical sex, present in the neural patterns of people, and 99% of the time overlapping with other physical parts as expected, just as chromosomes mostly overlap with genitals that overlap with hormone output. 

It's called *gender* identity rather than *sex* identity, because our ability to measure someone's brain is finite, we mostly identify what's inside your brain, based on how you express it. 

When someone who was assigned at birth as a man, starts saying "I am a woman", we guess that it most of the time seems to have something with their originally assigned birth being flawed in the first place, and bits of their brain were biologically female all along. But we still can't reliably measure that, so we keep assigning people based on their genitals, and treat their gender identity as a "mental" trait that sometimes surprisingly counters that.  

And yes, the specific details of gender expresson are socially constructed. Someone with gender dysphoria who rejects being called "she", uses social constructs to assert their identity. But that doesn't mean the identity itself is cultural.

 If someone was abused by a parent who used to call them "sweethart", and that person develops PTSD and starts treating the word "sweethart" coming even from you, as a triggering effect, that causes panic attacks, sweating, vomiting, etc, it doesn't help for you to point out that actually the word "sweethart" is just a part of language, and an arbitrary set of sounds, and they shouldn't have a strong biological reaction to it.  Because while the specific word is arbitrary, it could have been anything else, PTSDs ability to connect amental short-circuit to a specific terminology, is a physically existing reality. 

Similarly, Someone with gender dyphoria who was assigned at birth as female, but feels the need to have a penis, an adam's apple, facial hair, etc, are following a part of their body's expectation of what their body should look like. But when they *also* want to be addressed as "he", and go to the male bathroom, and dress as a male, they are expressing social constructs that might as well be totally the other way around, nevertheless, the brain has a habit of connecting it's biological needs to the social constructs that it experiences.

message:
Consent can be vitiated in law after the fact. This is not something new. We have been operating on this legal principle for many decades. For instance, if the consent was acquired in a way that undermines it's freely given nature then it can be vitiated. This occurs in situations where the consenting party was under duress, underage, mistaken as to the identity of the person they had sex with, and so on. 

If you are implying that people are saying that freely given consent can be rescinded after the entire sexual encounter has ended, you're going to have to source that because I have never heard anyone seriously suggest such an idea.
The under duress thing has been way out of proportion and I want more strict guidelines about when that comes into play.
"@inclusive-nickel, you're missing the point! I'm not talking about duress, I'm saying consent can be revoked mid-act, like a 'time-out' for adults. Ever heard of 'safe words'? 😏"
"@inclusive-nickel, dear, you're as old as the hills and still haven't heard about 'time-outs' in adult playtime? Bless your heart! 😂"

You are getting too wrapped up in how many days a teacher works. 

The sheer amount of effort that goes into teaching is insane. Its rocket science. Its the hardest thing to do--to teach young kids concepts on a level they will understand, which is hard to do as an adult. Any parent will confirm that. Then you stick them in a room wirh 25-30 kids, all who learn differently.

I was a teacher for a year. I taught at a middle school in a poor, urban neighborhood. I was the founding 8th grade history teacher which meanr i had to pick a textbook, design a syllabus, create the entire years worth of units, create lesson plans, grade homework, and teach classes. I was up most days at 5, and up until 11. Now, i also had to teach kids who spoke different languages, i had to teach kids with OOD, i had to teach kids with severe psychological distress. If you know anything about human behavior, you know that a fearful person is very difficult to work with. How are kids supposed to learn, when they slready know their life could end at any time? Literally some kids i taught saw neighbors get shot in front of their homes. How do kids go to school after that? And how much skill does it take to teach those kids? In many jobs, you have one job. Teachers wear many, many hats. Kids trust them for advice, for approval, for education, for a future, for protection. In school shootings, teachers have died to save their kids. 

It is the bedrock job of society. The education system determines a countrys future. Thats it. You want to be the best country you have to have the best education. If you want to pay teachers less, pay them less. Check out oklahoma and see how that has worked out. 

So dont get hung up on the number of days. If you want teachers to work summers they will, at a cost. Its the most basic job of a society and one of the toughest. There really shouldnt be a price we wouldnt pay to have quality teachers.

I think, the thing you are missing here, is that they are *relatively* underpaid.

The unfortunate aspect of government jobs, is that while they are enticing because of their non-pay elements they are overwhelmingly underpaid compared to private sector work.

When we are talking about teachers, this is problematic because we don't want to pay someone to babysit our kids, we want everyone to have a quality education so that we can have an educated workforce. 

If you don't provide incentives for teachers to stay in the profession you run into problems.

message: No sex is a deal breaker.  Period.  End of story. Full stop.  If my significant other is unilaterally deciding to "close up shop", I'm going to get my comforts someplace else regardless of the "marriage vows".  The door swings both ways and has nothing to do with "TRP".

> It is narcissistic to break an agreement, then be mad that the other person decided to break it too. Sex with a husband/wife is part of a marriage.

If you read marriage vows, many specifically include not cheating: "forsaking all others, "faithful to you", etc. I have yet to read one that specifically reads "I will continue to fuck my partner throughout this marriage." You could argue that "to cherish" includes "to fuck," but at the same time "to cherish" clearly includes respecting the decision to not have sex so that's not the strongest argument. 

Back to the hypothetical - should the rich man not be angry he was stolen from because he knew it was a possibility and it was his fault he was stolen from (because he was rich)?

PS - you might be better off posting a CMV of your TRP tendencies, you'd get more targeted responses.

OK you can't list dissemination of ideas and any and all media as "multiculturism." That's clearly not what I'm talking about. Using your logic, fire is multicultural.

>t's also worth pointing out that we have yet to see a society that has achieved the end goal of multiculturalism, which is a pluralistic society that has removed cultural and racial barriers to success

A couple problems with this: 

1) Communists say the same thing about communism. Your perfect heterogeneity, along with perfect communism, can never exist.

2) You speak nothing of the transition. This will take at least 100s of years, what will the impact of automation be between now and then? 

3) Is perfect heterogeneity even desireable? I thought we like "diversity?" Which is it?


I will read you papers though, I think those could be a valuable contribution.

If fractionalization is the worst multiculturalism has to offer, I will gladly take it over the atrocities committed in the name of ethnic nationalism. I would argue that multiculturalism offers an enriching experience that ethnic nationalism simply can't offer. For example, food is an obvious plus. I live in a very multicultural area. I can walk down the street and find a places that offer American, Mexican, Chinese, Mediterranean, Italian, Indian, Korean, African, and Japanese cuisines. In an ethnically homogeneous nation I would never have these options. Do you listen to music? Unless you listen to strictly classical music from the 1700's or prior, the music you love is a product of multiculturalism. You like Star Wars? Multiculturalism. The Matrix? Multiculturalism. The Lion King? Multiculturalism. There are also plenty of studies that show that ethnic diversity in itself [does not increase conflict] (https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/american-political-science-review/article/div-classtitleethnicity-insurgency-and-civil-wardiv/B1D5D0E7C782483C5D7E102A61AD6605) nor does it necessarily result in [poor public goods provisions.] (https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/american-political-science-review/article/div-classtitleeconomic-versus-cultural-differences-forms-of-ethnic-diversity-and-public-goods-provisiondiv/1A668817F0A20FE9FF613B2247654A0E) It's also worth pointing out that we have yet to see a society that has achieved the end goal of multiculturalism, which is a pluralistic society that has removed cultural and racial barriers to success.

message:
> Even the term cis-sexism itself is debatable as it was coined by a single person, Julia Serano, to suit her narrative.

Why does that make the term debatable? Most terms presumably had to be used by *someone* for the first time, but what's so controversial about this one? 

It describes transphobia as a matter of sexism against trans people, in other words, a sexist bias in favor of cis people. Cis-sexism. It seems rather straightforward. 

> In a minority of cases would they be used in this way. It's become a catch all phrase for something that offends people. See this video

Alternatively, look at any reddit thread making fun of anyone who claims something is racist or sexist, by claiming that they must feel "TRIGGERED". 

That word has undoubtedly slipped down into the buzzword trash heap, but if you were out to pin the blame for that on social movement, you could just as easily spot reactionaries going out of their way to dig up a handful of obscure tumblr posts/youtube videos to turn them into a meme, than on the general concept of "modern social justice".

PTSD does exist, and it's an established fact that traumatic flashbacks can be triggered by various stimuli, often by content that imitates the circumstances of the trauma. (slurs, graphic descriptions of violence, etc). 

If your problem is with the people who use the term incorrectly, then you could blame most of the internet, regardless of ideology. If your problem is that "*The idea of trigger warnings destroys free speech*", that's an issue regardless of how widely people are using them.

"@olive-gallery, I understand your point about the origin of terms, but I think the issue with 'cis-sexism' is that it can alienate cis people from the conversation about trans issues. It's not about the term's validity, but how it's perceived and used. As for trigger warnings, I agree that they're not inherently bad, but when they're used to silence or censor, that's when it becomes a problem. Let's not throw the baby out with the bathwater, yeah?"

>genes are found all over the world, in different concentrations, and this is just random

What's your background/understanding of genetics? I'm not insulting you, I'm genuinely asking so I can figure out if we're on the same page here or if we're talking about two different things and miscommunicating. 

>are all part of the rest of humanity and who all share genes together

We don't all share the same genes. I'm not sure what you mean by this. 

>so the anser is if someone has gene a b c, so do billions of others, so what? it doesn't say anything about that person

No, billions of others do not, that's the whole point. Only *some* humans have the gene for sickle cell anemia. If you cross reference enough of these genes you can assert that the person in question (ABC) is likely from Africa. 100% certain? No. But 23 and Me doesn't claim to offer 100% certainty.

I've got a hypothetical that's basically a simplified version of what these genetic tests do (as far as I understand them). It is based off of a statement you made in another comment:

>As for sickle cell: Indeed it is true that some people in S and West Africa have a gene for sickle cell as a defense to malaria ... however that gene is not limited to Africa and can be found in Latin America too.

Okay, image you have Gene A which is found in Africa and Latin America.

Then you have Gene B which is found in Africa and China. 

Then you have Gene C which is from Africa and Australia. 

If a person has genes A, B, and C, where is that person from?

"@oriented-stage, I think you're oversimplifying it. Genes A, B, and C aren't equally prevalent in all those regions. The combination of all three would likely indicate African ancestry, as that's where all three are most common. But I'm no geneticist, just going by what I've read."
"@flavorful-seasoning, You're right, I did oversimplify. I'm no expert either, just trying to understand. Thanks for the correction!"
"@oriented-stage, I think it's cool how you're trying to understand this stuff! But yeah, @flavorful-seasoning is right, it's not as simple as just adding up the genes. 🤔"
"@oriented-stage, Oh, look who's suddenly humble! 🙄"

message:
It's allowed, but still suspicious.  

To take an example from that blog post, imagine if I started talking about all the Chinese robbers that there are, and the economic and emotional harm that Chinese criminals are causing across the world.  You'd probably want to know why I'm talking about *Chinese* crime, much like I want to know why OP's talking about *women's* double standards.
Maybe he's taking it one topic at a time, and women is the topic for this one. Is it not allowed to be discussed?
